question: In Arkansas you will be arrested for driving while intoxicated (DWI) with a blood or breath alcohol concentration of:
choices: Over 0.05%
Over 0.06%
Over 0.07%
0.08% or more
explanation: 0.08% is the DWI threshold — and if you're in a crash or serious traffic offense, you can be charged with DWI between 0.04% and 0.08%. First conviction: $150–$1,000 fine, up to 1 year in jail, and a 120-day license suspension.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Alcohol and the Law

question: A driver UNDER 21 can be arrested for Underage Driving Under the Influence at a BAC of:
explanation: Arkansas's underage DUI threshold is just 0.02% — roughly one drink. A conviction can mean license revocation until age 21 and a fine up to $2,000.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 75 — Underage Driving Under the Influence

question: If a law enforcement officer instructs you to take an alcohol test (breath, blood, or urine), you:
choices: Usually may refuse the test, and typically no penalty will be applied to you
Are compelled by law to take it — refusing can cost you your driver's license
May personally pick whichever one of the tests you happen to most prefer taking
Typically have to take the test only if you were actually involved in a crash
explanation: The officer chooses the test, and you are compelled by law to take it. You can lose your driver's license if you refuse — separate from any DWI charge.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Alcohol and the Law

question: The ONLY thing that will sober you up is:
choices: Time
Gum
A cold shower
Fresh air
explanation: The body rids itself of alcohol at about one drink per hour. Coffee, fresh air, exercise, and cold showers will NOT help — only time will.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Body Metabolism

question: A driver with an Arkansas Instruction Permit or Learner's License must be accompanied by:
choices: A licensed driver who is at least 16
A licensed adult who is at least 19 years old
A licensed driver at least 18 years old
A licensed driver who is at least 21 years old
explanation: Permit and Learner's License holders must drive with a licensed driver 21 or older (beside the driver for a permit), and all passengers must wear seat belts.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Types of Graduated Driver's Licenses

question: For drivers holding an Arkansas Learner's or Intermediate License, cell phone use while driving is:
choices: Usually allowed when the phone is fully hands-free
Allowed for short calls but generally not for texting
Prohibited by law except for an emergency
Allowed while the car is stopped at red lights
explanation: For Learner's and Intermediate license holders, ANY cell phone use — talking or texting — is prohibited except in an emergency. Under 18, even hands-free devices are not allowed.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Graduated Licenses; p. 46 — Distracted Driving Law

question: The Arkansas Intermediate License is issued to drivers age 16–18. The restriction is that the driver:
choices: May not drive on the interstate after 9 p.m. on weekends
Must be accompanied by a licensed driver 21+ until the 6-month restricted period is complete
May drive only directly to and from school, and only during the daylight hours of day
May not, on residential streets, drive faster than 45 mph
explanation: Intermediate License holders are restricted for 6 months from permit issuance (or until requirements are met at 16): a licensed driver 21 or older must accompany them, all passengers must be belted, and no cell phone use is allowed.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Intermediate License

question: One 'drink' is considered 1.5 oz of 80-proof liquor, a 6-oz glass of wine, or:
choices: A 12-oz beer
A single 16-oz beer
A large 24-oz beer
A 32-oz mug of beer
explanation: 1.5 oz of liquor = 6 oz of wine = 12 oz of beer. Specialty drinks can equal several normal drinks. Even two drinks may cause impairment and can get you arrested.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Body Metabolism

question: The FIRST driving ability affected by alcohol is:
choices: Your eyesight
Reaction time
Coordination
Judgment
explanation: Judgment goes first — which is exactly why people keep drinking past their limit and decide they're 'fine to drive.' By the time you feel the alcohol, your judgment is already severely affected.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 73-74 — Alcohol Impairment

question: A first DWI conviction in Arkansas can bring:
choices: A verbal warning from the arresting officer, plus a modest court filing fee
A $150–$1,000 fine, up to one year in jail, and a 120-day license suspension
A $50 fine, two days of jail, and a 30-day license suspension
A few hours of court-ordered community service and a small fine of forty dollars
explanation: First conviction: $150–$1,000 plus court costs, up to a year in jail, and a 120-day suspension. Penalties get much worse for repeat offenses.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Alcohol and the Law

question: A person under 21 convicted of merely PURCHASING or POSSESSING alcohol (not driving):
choices: Faces a possible fine but usually keeps their driving privileges intact
Usually receives a written warning letter mailed to their home address
Will have their driving privileges suspended, revoked, or disqualified
Pays a small $25 fine and keeps driving
explanation: Under-21 alcohol purchase or possession costs your driving privileges in Arkansas — even with no car involved. Underage DUI itself can mean license revoked until 21 and a $2,000 fine.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 75 — Underage Driving Under the Influence

question: An Arkansas Instruction Permit requires the driver to be at least 14, pass the knowledge and vision tests, and is valid for:
choices: 1 week
1 year
5 days
2 years
explanation: The permit is issued for 2 years from the date of the passed knowledge exam. The permit holder must drive with a licensed driver 21+ in the seat beside them.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Instruction Permit

question: Many prescription and over-the-counter drugs (cold, allergy, pain medicine):
choices: Can impair your driving like alcohol — check the label or ask your doctor or pharmacist before driving
Usually have little or no effect on your ability to drive, so most people can safely take them and drive
Generally impair your ability to drive only when taken together with a fairly large amount of alcohol
Are usually illegal for most people to take at any time before they get behind the wheel and drive off
explanation: Drowsiness-causing medicines impair reflexes, judgment, and vision. Mixing them with alcohol multiplies the effects. Check labels for the 'do not operate machinery' warning.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 75 — Other Types of Drugs and Driving

question: Alcohol is involved in approximately what percentage of fatal traffic crashes?
choices: About 50%
About 65%
About 80%
explanation: About 38% of traffic crashes in which someone is killed involve alcohol. There is no safe alcohol concentration for driving — and new drivers are affected even more than experienced ones.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 73 — Drinking and Driving

question: When going out where alcohol will be served, the study guide recommends:
choices: Eating a very large meal beforehand so that you can drink a good deal and still safely drive yourself home
Drinking several cups of strong black coffee right before you get into the car to start driving home
Going with two or more people so one can be the designated driver — or using public transportation or a cab
Sticking to the quiet back roads on the way home and simply driving very slowly and carefully
explanation: Plan ahead: a designated driver, a cab, or public transportation. Food slows alcohol absorption but will NOT prevent you from getting intoxicated.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Body Metabolism

question: Which statement about alcohol and new drivers is TRUE?
choices: Experienced drivers, over many years of driving, build up a strong tolerance to most of alcohol's effects
Having just one single drink will rarely cause noticeable impairment in an otherwise healthy adult driver
Alcohol usually begins to affect a driver only once they have gone above the 0.08% legal BAC limit for adults
Every driver is affected by alcohol, and new drivers are affected even MORE because they are still learning
explanation: There is no safe alcohol concentration for driving. Even one drink affects judgment — and new drivers, still building skills, are affected more than veterans. Two drinks can mean an arrest.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 73-74 — Alcohol Impairment

question: To receive an Arkansas Learner's License, the applicant cannot have been involved in a crash or convicted of a serious traffic violation within:
choices: The preceding 6 months
The preceding thirty days
The preceding 12 months
The preceding three years
explanation: A clean record for the preceding six months — no crashes, no serious traffic convictions — is required for the Learner's License (ages 14-16, after the knowledge, vision, and skills tests).
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Learner's License

question: Where is an Intermediate License holder's restricted driving period recorded?
choices: In a separate document held by the local court
On the reverse side of the driver's license
Inside the state's central driver computer system
On the vehicle's registration card
explanation: The period of restricted time is recorded on the back of the Intermediate License — officers (and parents) can check exactly when the supervised period ends.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Intermediate License

question: Pep pills, 'uppers,' and diet pills can make a driver feel more alert for a short time. Later, they can cause:
choices: A lasting state of sharp focus and steady, reliable alertness
A real, long-lasting improvement in reaction time
Nervousness, dizziness, inability to concentrate, and poor vision
Usually no harmful after-effects of any sort for the driver later on
explanation: Stimulants wear off into nervousness, dizziness, and poor concentration and vision. No drug increases concentration safely — and none stops impairment from alcohol.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 75 — Other Types of Drugs and Driving

question: After you drink alcohol, about how long does it take to reach your brain and begin affecting you?
choices: Instantly, as you swallow
About 2 to 3 full hours
About 15 to 40 minutes
About 5 to 10 seconds
explanation: Alcohol is absorbed from the stomach and small intestine into the blood, reaching the brain in about 15 to 40 minutes. The first ability it affects is judgment.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 73 — Alcohol Impairment

question: About how fast does the body get rid of alcohol?
choices: Roughly three full drinks per hour for a healthy adult
Roughly one drink per hour — and nothing speeds it up
Drinking a few cups of black coffee can double the rate
Most of it leaves your system soon after you stop drinking
explanation: The liver breaks down only about 0.5 to 1 ounce of alcohol per hour — roughly one drink. Coffee, fresh air, food, and cold showers will NOT speed it up; only time will.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Body Metabolism

question: When an officer asks you to take a chemical test for alcohol, who decides which test (breath, blood, urine, or saliva) is given?
choices: The law enforcement officer chooses the test
You personally choose the test you prefer
A judge decides which test later in court
A warrant is generally required before testing
explanation: The officer chooses the test. Breath alcohol concentration (BrAC) is measured from your breath; blood alcohol concentration (BAC) from blood. You are compelled by law to take it — refusing can cost you your license.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Alcohol and the Law

question: Penalties for drinking and driving in Arkansas can include higher insurance rates, jail, loss of license, and:
choices: A simple written warning from the court
A defensive-driving class coupon
Lower car insurance rates
A required ignition interlock device
explanation: People who drink and drive risk higher insurance rates, loss of license, jail, and possible ignition interlock devices (which won't let the car start if they detect alcohol). Penalties get worse for repeat offenses.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 73 — Drinking and Driving

question: You are in a traffic crash and your BAC is 0.05% — below the usual 0.08% limit. In Arkansas you:
choices: Usually cannot be charged with much at all there, since your reading is comfortably below the usual 0.08% legal limit for adult drivers
Will usually face only a minor parking-type fine for the crash itself, because a 0.05% reading is generally not treated as a DWI in Arkansas
Can still be charged with DWI — a BAC between 0.04% and 0.08% can bring a charge if you're in a crash or serious traffic offense
Are generally cleared of an alcohol-related charge, because under the law it usually counts as DWI only at a reading of 0.08% or higher
explanation: 0.08% is the usual DWI threshold, but if you're involved in a crash or a serious traffic offense you can be charged with DWI at a BAC between 0.04% and 0.08%.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Alcohol and the Law

question: According to the study guide, studies show drivers who use marijuana:
choices: Tend to slow themselves down, pay much closer attention, and drive far more carefully and cautiously than other drivers do
Make more mistakes, have more trouble adjusting to glare, and get more traffic violations than other drivers
Are essentially unaffected behind the wheel and drive about as well as anyone else who happens to be out on the road
Usually show only a slightly slower reaction time when they happen to be driving late at night
explanation: Illegal drugs impair driving. Studies show marijuana users make more mistakes, have more trouble adjusting to glare, and are arrested for traffic violations more than other drivers.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 75 — Other Types of Drugs and Driving

question: Eating a big meal before you drink alcohol will:
choices: Slow how fast alcohol is absorbed, but NOT prevent you from becoming intoxicated
Usually keep you sober even after you have had a good many alcoholic drinks
Usually make it safe enough for you to drive yourself home a short while later
Lower your blood alcohol level and reliably keep it below the legal driving limit
explanation: Food slows the rate at which alcohol enters the blood, but it will not prevent intoxication. The only thing that lowers your alcohol level is time.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 74 — Body Metabolism

question: As the amount of alcohol in your body increases, it:
choices: Improves your reaction time and steadily sharpens your driving skills, getting better the more that you drink
Affects mainly your eyesight and little else, leaving the rest of your driving skills largely intact and fine
Has little real effect on your ability to drive until right about the moment that you happen to reach the 0.08% limit
Worsens your judgment and decreases your skills — including judging distances, speeds, and the movement of other vehicles
explanation: More alcohol means worse judgment and weaker driving skills: slower reflexes, blurred vision, and trouble judging distances, speeds, and how other vehicles are moving — plus the danger of losing control.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 73 — Alcohol Impairment
